cards.title: Local AI is Exploding, But Collaboration is Broken: The Missing Link for Team AI Workflows
cards.thesis: More people are running powerful AI models (like ChatGPT, but on your own computer) directly on their machines, and even orchestrating multiple AI 'agents' to work together. This shift means a lot of creative and technical work is happening locally, but there's a huge gap in how teams share, review, and collaborate on the outputs of these private AI setups.

evidence.text: Nativ lets you run cutting-edge AI models right on your Mac, which is getting a lot of attention.

Someone built 'Burmese,' a cross-platform tool because they were running many parallel coding AI agents and needed a single place to track their progress instead of checking multiple terminals. It connects to AI sessions already running on your machine.
A B2B marketing agency grew to $1.5M ARR in 6 months by going all-in on being an 'AI-native' agency, not just using AI tools, but wiring AI into their core workflows.
People are actively comparing new AI models like OpenCode and Kimi K3 against established ones like Claude Code, looking for the best options.

cards.opportunity: The surge in running powerful AI models locally and orchestrating multiple agents (which are like little AI programs working together) is creating a huge bottleneck: sharing and collaborating on their outputs. While individual agent control is emerging, the real pain is trying to get team feedback on code, designs, or content generated by your private, local AI setup without resorting to endless copy-pasting. Someone needs to build a lightweight, secure 'sharing layer' that lets you push agent outputs from your local machine to a shared workspace for review and comments, making team iteration on AI-generated work seamless, and you could probably hook it up with a simple local agent that watches a folder and pushes changes.

cards.title: Stop Blindly Trusting Your Local AI — A New Market Is Opening Up
cards.thesis: Builders are increasingly deploying powerful AI agents for critical tasks like algorithmic trading or code analysis, often running them locally for privacy and control. However, there's a growing pain point around truly trusting these agents (automated programs that act on your behalf) to do exactly what they're told, requiring better ways to monitor, authorize, and verify their actions.

evidence.text: Local AI, native to your Mac. Chat, serve, monitor, and connect MLX models from one macOS app.
Agent Execution Partnership AEE is an open-source control plane that ensures every AI agent action is authorized before it runs, observable while it runs, and verifiable after it completes.
Machine Genome is an open identity and provenance protocol for models, agents, harnesses, datasets, and the artifacts that connect them.
Orchestrate AI agents to find real vulnerabilities in code.
The0 – self-hosted runtime for trading bots. I started building algorithmic trading bots, but running them on local machines was not reliable.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's building powerful AI agents, but the real challenge isn't just making them smart, it's *trusting* them, especially when they're running locally on your machine for sensitive tasks like managing investments or checking code. Builders are actively seeking 'control planes' to ensure agent actions are authorized and verifiable. Imagine a super simple, local-first dashboard that plugs into tools like Blaizzy/nativ, letting users easily monitor, approve, and audit every action their personal AI agent takes, giving them peace of mind and full control over their digital co-pilots.

cards.title: Niche No More: How AI Can Help You Own the 'Human Expertise' Market
cards.thesis: While AI is rapidly integrating into daily workflows and powering new products like K-12 tutors, there's a growing sentiment among builders of feeling 'lazy and dumb' due to over-reliance on AI. At the same time, a significant number of people are actively seeking out 'favorite blogs not about AI,' longing for the '2009 era of blogging about personal projects or super niche factoids.' This shows a clear hunger for authentic, human-driven expertise amidst the AI noise.

evidence.text: Bloomy (YC S26) launched an AI-powered mastery-learning platform for K-12 students, providing an AI tutor and adaptive curriculum.

Many people are asking for their 'favorite blogs not about AI,' missing the '2009 era of blogging about personal projects or super niche factoids' because their preferred bloggers have stopped or shifted topics.

One frontend engineer shared that they stopped fighting AI over-reliance and built a workflow around it, but now feels 'lazy and dumb' compared to the pre-AI era, preferring to spend two minutes explaining to AI rather than manually writing code.
Qwen-Image-3.0 was announced, promising 'Rich Content, Authentic Details, Deep Knowledge' from an AI model.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's integrating AI, but many builders are feeling 'lazy and dumb' and people are actively seeking out 'favorite blogs not about AI' with 'super niche factoids.' The move is to empower niche experts to produce and distribute their unique, human-driven insights using AI as an *enhancer*, not a replacement. You could build a simple web tool that helps an expert curate, summarize (with AI), and then *heavily human-edit* specific niche content, then publishes it with a distinct, personal voice that stands out from generic AI slop. Think 'Substack for deep, AI-augmented human expertise' — the first person to nail that will own the frustrated-expert market.

cards.title: Major Security Hole Found in Popular Web App Tool – Is Your Product Vulnerable?
cards.thesis: A significant security flaw has been exposed in Fastjson, a widely used tool for handling data in many web applications (think of it like the language apps use to talk to each other). This flaw allows attackers to potentially run their own code on a vulnerable app's server (meaning they can take over your app), even if standard defenses are in place. Thousands of apps are likely exposed right now.

evidence.text: A Docker lab and exploit payload were released, demonstrating how to remotely execute code in apps using Fastjson versions 1.2.66-1.2.83, even when common security settings are turned off. This shows how serious and widespread the vulnerability is.

New exploit tools are out that work across all major versions of Java (JDK 8, 17, 21, and 25) for Fastjson 1.2.68-1.2.83, and they can even check multiple website URLs at once. This means the exploit is robust and easy to deploy.
Another team has also released a tool specifically for exploiting Fastjson 1.2.68-1.2.83, confirming the severity and active development around this vulnerability.

cards.opportunity: Security researchers just dropped a bunch of new, easy-to-use tools to exploit a major vulnerability in Fastjson, a super common data tool in many apps. Everyone from small startups to big companies using Java apps is scrambling to figure out if they're exposed. You could build a dead-simple online scanner service that takes a website URL, tells them if they're vulnerable to this specific Fastjson issue, and gives them a one-click report to send to their dev team, all built on top of the open-source scanners that just dropped.

cards.title: Privacy-First Analytics: Where AI Turns Data Into Dollars (Not Just Numbers)
cards.thesis: Indie builders and aspiring founders want to understand their website visitors without compromising user privacy, moving away from traditional analytics tools. A new wave of 'privacy-friendly' web analytics is emerging, with some even incorporating AI to generate insights, but they often fall short of providing truly actionable advice for making money.

evidence.text: Reignat offers web analytics that respects user privacy and provides real-time AI-powered insights.
Kobbe provides privacy-friendly web analytics, focusing on helping users track traffic and revenue.

cards.opportunity: Most 'privacy-friendly analytics' tools just give you numbers, which doesn't solve the core problem for builders: how to actually make more money from their site. Instead of generic analytics, imagine a tool that specifically watches the conversion path on your landing page (think a v0 site or Gumroad product page) using privacy-first methods. Then, its AI brain could tell you, 'Hey, visitors from Twitter are dropping off after the first paragraph, try adding a short video explainer here,' or 'Your pricing table isn't converting for users coming from Product Hunt, maybe simplify the tiers.' You'd be offering truly actionable, revenue-focused advice, not just data, by integrating directly with payment platforms (like Stripe or Gumroad) to track actual sales against visitor behavior.
